title: ComfyUI Qwen VL3: Creating Prompts from Images and Text (Ep03)
description:
In this ComfyUI tutorial, you’ll learn how to use Qwen VL3 to generate high-quality prompts from images, text, or both. This episode shows how to analyze images inside ComfyUI and turn them into detailed prompts, tags, descriptions, and creative text that can be fed directly into image generation workflows.

What you’ll learn in this tutorial:
- How Qwen VL3 works inside ComfyUI (image + text → smart text output)
- Updating the ComfyUI Easy Installer to access the latest nodes
- Installing and verifying the QwenVL custom node
- Safetensors vs GGUF models (speed, VRAM usage, and quality)
- Choosing between 2B, 4B, Q4, Q8, and F16 models
- Generating prompts, tags, descriptions, stories, and analyses from images
- Using custom formulas to control prompt style and output
- Turning image descriptions into text-to-image prompts automatically
- Combining multiple images to generate consistent characters and scenes
- Managing VRAM issues and optimizing performance
- When to use local models v
transcript: In this episode, we're turning images and text into smart prompts inside Comfy UI.  I'll show you simple practical examples and we'll see how this works using QnVL3.  Are you ready for some bunny wisdom?  This infographic breaks down how QnVL3 works in Comfy UI.  You start by giving it an image or multiple images, plus optional text instructions.  QnVL3 then understands what's in the image and follows your text to analyze it.  Finally, you get useful text output, such as descriptions, prompts, or helpful explanations.  I just want to make sure you have the latest version of the Comfy UI Easy installer first,  so you have access to the latest nodes.  So, let's update the Easy installer first using this BAT file, which is quite fast.  When you see press any key to exit, you can press Enter or any key to close it.  After that, I want you to update Comfy UI, including the nodes,  since we have a custom node that I want to use in this episode.  It gets installed automatically with the Easy installer, and it will allow us to run the QnVL  large language models. Let me go to Manager and then to Custom Nodes Manager.  If we search for QnVL, you will see that this custom node is already installed.  If you are using a different version of Comfy UI, you may need to install this node.  In that case, you should see an install button instead of uninstall, just like I have for other  nodes. This is the author of the node, and if we click on the node title, we can go directly to the  GitHub page for that node. It is always a good idea to check for updates to see what new models are  supported and what has been updated. So now you have everything you need, because the Easy installer  makes things very simple. Let's get to practice now. Double click on the Canvas and search for QnVL.  You have here a bunch of nodes that you can try, but today I will use only the first two nodes.  So this is the QnVL node, and we also have another node with GGUF version in the name.  The main difference is that one loads normal safe tensors models and the other one GGUF models.  You can see that we can load quite a few models, but the bigger the number in front of the B,  the smarter it is. At the same time, it is bigger in size and needs more V RAM.  2B means 2 billion parameters. A parameter is basically a learned value inside the model.  You can think of parameters as the model's memory and learn knowledge.  More parameters mean the model has more capacity to understand patterns and details.  Fewer parameters mean the model is faster and lighter, but less detailed.  So when you see numbers like 4B, that means 4 billion parameters. That is essentially the size of  the model's brain. You'll see two types of models here. Instruct and thinking.  I'll use instruct today because it's great for generating clear prompts and descriptions,  while thinking is better for slower, deeper reasoning, and more complex analysis.  And for the GGUF, we have the same naming. The models are just in GGUF format,  which is a quantized version, better for low V RAM and smaller size.  I prefer to use GGUF for this, and you will see why in a few minutes.  Let me delete the GGUF node and start with the first one.  We can see it has a response output. This will be a text output, or a string as developers  call it. If we drag a link from it and release, we can search for a node that matches that output.  You can see it is now sorted and only shows string nodes that have a string input.  Search for Preview and select this preview as a text node. We can move it closer and make it larger.  Now let's look again at the model name. I will select the smaller one, the 2B version.  For this node, we do not have to download the model manually, because the node will  download it automatically for us. Here you can add a custom formula and a prompt,  but since it can only generate text, you have to say to give you a prompt.  If you do not mention prompt, it thinks you want to generate an image, and it will say it cannot  do images. Now when I run this simple workflow, you will see it stays for a long time.  That is because it first needs to download the model, then load it, and then generate with that  model. The download only happens the first time you use it. You can see here it says fetching  10 files. In those 10 files, there is the model and some extra instructions it needs to be  recognized. Once the download is finished and the model is loaded, you get a prompt,  similar to what you get with chat GPT or Gemini. If I run it again, you can see that now the model  is already downloaded and loaded, and it takes around 6 seconds to generate a prompt.  In the result, we got more than just the prompt. We also got some extra text, so let's say  only give me the prompt, and now you can see the result is only the prompt.  So now we know that depending on the model, the bigger it is, the smarter it is, but also  slower. Then we have quantization, which depends on the video card. By default, it is set to max.  Attention mode I usually leave on auto. Here we also have different formula presets. If I remove  what is here, it will use that detailed description preset. For example, let's search for a load  image node and connect it to the image input here. Then you can use this right arrow to navigate  through your images. If you have the easy installer, you should have this bunny image. If not,  just take a screenshot of it. Then we can select different templates from here. For example,  if I select tags, I will get a list of tags. For me, this node was kind of slow with this 2B  model, which is the smallest, so I decided to give the GGUF node a try. This lets us load GGUF  models that can be smarter and smaller in size, so they fit better in memory. Let me redo the  connections here. Now if we look at the models for 4B, the F16 is the biggest, the Q8 is a medium size,  and the Q4 is the smaller size. Let's try the small size first. If we look in the console,  we can see that it is downloading that model as well. We can also see where it is downloading it.  It says it is in the comfy UI models folder inside the LLM folder. Once that model is downloaded  and loaded, we get our prompt. Let's check the downloaded models so we can understand this better.  In the models folder, we have this LLM folder. Inside, we have a folder that corresponds to the  first node we used, the one with 2B in the name. You can see that among the other files it needs  to run, it has this safe tensors model that is over 4GB in size. If we now look at the GGUF model  instead, we can see the 4B version. It has more parameters, but it is just a little over 2GB in size.  So it is smarter and smaller than the normal model, and it loads faster.  Since my video card supports it, I like to use the medium size Q8 version. When I run it,  it will download that model as well in the same folder.  You can see this one is over 4GB in size. For my tests, this one gave me better results  compared to the Q4 version, but it is a little slower. As you can see, it depends on your computer  and your video card. Always try different models and see which one runs at an acceptable speed  on your system, then stick with that one. For max tokens, you decide how long you expect the answer  to be. If you want a long answer and your tokens are set too low, the answer might get cut off.  You can see that the bigger the number of tokens, the longer the answer you get,  but it is also a bit slower. You can see how the time increased when I increased the tokens.  Even then, it is still about three times faster than the previous Safe Tensors model we tried.  That is why, in this case, GGUF was faster for me, and that is the one I use.  So for the tags preset, we get tags that can be used for all kinds of things.  Maybe you need them for Lura training, or you have a product and you need keywords for Etsy or  something similar. Then we have simple description for when you want something short.  But with all these new AI models, it usually works better with long prompts.  That is why most of the time I use detailed description, or I add my own custom formula to  generate what I need. Ultra detailed description is also good when you want something very specific,  but it depends on the model. Some AI models can get lost if there's too much information and only  taken to account the first part of the prompt. Cinematic description is for when you want a more  cinematic look. Detailed analysis makes the model describe the image in a structured, in-depth way,  breaking it down into subject, environment, lighting, composition, and visual details instead of  just a short caption. Video summary is like a description of a video. We can add a load video  node, like this one from Video Helper Suite. Let me remove the load image node and move this one  closer. Now we can connect it to the video input. You can upload a video here, select a video,  and when we run it, we get a text describing what is happening in that video. I am not sure if it  can interpret the audio or only the visual part since we have that image output there.  Let me remove that and add the load image node again and check what else we can try.  Short story creates a story based on the image. This can be useful if you create social media posts  and want a story to go with the image, or if you do educational content for kids and want a small  story to help them remember the subject better. So play with these options and see what fits your  needs. Like I said, I mostly use detailed description or a custom formula made with chat GPT.  Here are some examples of custom formulas. For example, I can ask it to write a short poem  inspired by the rabbit in the image, so it captures its personality. The result is a poem,  exactly like we asked. Now let's combine these nodes with an image generation workflow.  We already have a few workflows from episode one, like the Z image workflow or even some  nunchaku ones from episode two. As you will see, this works with any image workflow. Let's say I  opened this 5a workflow for Z image turbo. Normally, we add our prompts in this text encoder node,  right? We could just copy the prompt from the previous workflow and paste it here,  but there must be a quicker way to do that. So let's double click on the canvas and add the node,  the GGUF1, since it is faster for me. Drag a link from it and add the preview as text node,  so we can see what prompt we get before we connect it to the Z image workflow. Then I want to  connect that text response to the text encoder. You can see it has a small dot in the top left  corner of that text field. Now that text field is disabled because it takes the information directly  from our node. Let's quickly write a prompt for a bunny walking in the forest. I was not sure why it  took so long, since I used it before, and then I noticed that by default it was using the F16  model, the big one. So I will change it to the Q8 version that I already have downloaded,  since it is smaller. Now I will run the workflow again. So we got this prompt for the bunny,  and that prompt goes into the text encoder. Then the generation starts, just like we saw in episode  one. And we get this bunny, which is a realistic one. But I want a cartoon style, so let me adjust  the prompt to say a cute cartoon bunny and try again. Now it looks much closer to what I had in mind.  Let's say I want a white bunny and a longer prompt. Now you can see the prompt is longer,  and we get the bunny result here. Let me remove that custom prompt and instead use the detailed  description preset. I will also add the load image node so we can generate a prompt from an image.  We connect that to the image input. Now when I generate, I get a long description, and the result  should be similar if the description is accurate to the uploaded image. Just to be clear,  this is not an image to image workflow. It does not take the image and modify it. It reads the  image, generates text from it, and based on that text, it creates something new. A different seed  will give you different results. Let's try another custom prompt. I want to analyze the image and  rewrite it as a long image prompt. I want to keep the pose and style, but replace the rabbit with  a panda. Now when I generate, I get this long prompt, but let's see if we actually get a panda.  The result is better than I expected. What do you think? It is quite cool, right? For a text to  image workflow, this works really well. For more control, we would need an edit model,  which will probably be released soon for Z image as well. Now let's replace the panda with a cat.  And now we get a cute cat. Here I added a longer formula to change the art style of the image  to a watercolor painting. Let's see if it works. The result is not bad. I wonder what else we can try?  Let's see if we can create an item in the same art style, like borrowing the art style from  the image and creating a car in that style. I'm not sure if this result represents the style  accurately, so let me try a different seed to see if we can get something better. This one  is much better. Different seeds can give you different results because the prompt changes slightly  each time. Let's try again. This one does not work. It is too different. Let's see what else we can  adjust in the prompt to get more consistent results. Let's force it to always be a cute cartoon car  so we guide the generation better. This one is really cute. I like it. Let's try again with a  different seed. Okay, that one is funny. I am having fun with these prompts based on the bunny  image. Let's see if we can make something more sci-fi. Maybe a device. We get this result,  which is kind of simple. Let's try a different seed. Yeah, this one is more cute. That works better.  Let's load another image. Maybe this photo of a dog. What I want to do now is add another image,  so I will duplicate the load image node and use a cat image for this one. You can see that if I  try to connect the cat image directly, it will disconnect the dog. So how do we add two or more  images there? There is a way. Let's search for a node called batch images. Now I can connect the  dog image to image 0 and the cat image to image 1 and so on. You can add multiple images like this.  Then we connect the image output to our smart node. Let's try a different prompt and see if we  can combine the images somehow. First, I ask it to analyze the images and identify and describe  each animal. Then I ask it to rewrite that as a long prompt with both the cat and the dog  together in the same scene. Let's see if it works. Obviously, the dog and the cat are not identical,  but the dog is quite similar. Many times we have an idea in our head and we do not know how to  explain it to get a good prompt. In cases like that, we can use images to generate more accurate prompts.  Maybe I add another rule here. Like the cat and dog should have a pink cat. Let's see if this works.  And it looks like it does. So we can get similar characters and mood from images,  which is useful when you do not know how to name a style or a subject. Now let's change the  cat to something else, like a bunny. What I want to do with this long prompt is make the dog have  the outfit of the bunny adapted for the dog with a hat, boots, and so on. We get this result for  the first seed. Let's try a few more seeds and see what we get. It looks pretty nice and fun to  play with, right? To recap really quickly, try different models and see what works best. If the  model is too big and you combine it with a heavy image generation model, everything might not fit  in VRAM and it will slow everything down. In that case, you can try a smaller image generation model  combined with a smaller LLM model so it fits better in memory. It happened a few times that  everything went slow, even workflows that used to be fast. A comfy UI restart fixed that for me.  Try different presets or your own custom prompts. Adjust the max tokens if needed. You can also  use a fixed seed if you want. That way it generates only once and then you can get multiple images  from the same prompt. Let's try one more thing before we end this episode. Let's say I open a  different workflow that I know is usually slower on my computer than Z image. Then I quickly add a  load image node, the quen node, and let's say I use the Q8 version. After that, I connect it to  the text encoder and also add a preview as text node so we can see the prompt result.  Now, because both models are big and I am recording and also have other programs open,  sometimes it can run out of VRAM or generate really slow. That is why it is important to adapt  the models depending on how much VRAM you have. This is one of the reasons I still often use chat  GPT in a different tab and just copy and paste prompts so I do not use VRAM from my computer.  You might get a message like this in the console, something like out of memory or allocation on  device. In some cases, you can try using a node like clean VRAM after the prompt is generated  and that might help. A better solution is to use a smaller model since smaller models need less  VRAM and leave more room for the main image model in memory. If you still do not have enough VRAM,  you can use a paid node from chat GPT. For example, I can add this node and redo the connections.  It works the same way as the previous node, but instead of using our VRAM, it uses the VRAM  from the server that provides access to that model. Right now, it uses credits per token.  We can choose a smaller model like GPT5 Nano. This costs under one credit. It shows zero,  but it is actually zero something. Let's add a formula here to generate a prompt and run it.  It says we need to sign in to use API nodes. I showed the steps in episode one. Just sign in quickly  with email or google and then you will have your user panel here. You can purchase credits from here.  I currently have 2007 credits, so let's see how many I have after generating. You can check the  status here. You can see when it is waiting for the server and so on. Even though it is paid,  you still have to wait for the connection and for the response to be generated and sent back.  It took about 16 seconds the first time to get that prompt. Now that prompt is used in the workflow,  and the result is similar to the reference image. If we check again, we still have the same number  of credits. Let me try a different image, like this man. The result looks like this. The  Quinn model is not the best for realism. I use it more for illustrations. Let's test it quickly  in the Z image workflow. I copied those three nodes. Now I open the Z image workflow  and paste them here. Then I connect it to the text encoder, adjust the size, and run it again.  Now we get this man. It captured the clothes, age, beard, and so on. If we check the credits again,  only now I lost one credit. So it is quite cheap to use a paid option like GPT-5 Nano.
